title: Remembering God's Deliverance
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Remembering God's Deliverance - Pastor Tito Lyro - Habakkuk 3_16-19.txt

outline.title: I. Context: Habakkuk's Complaint and God's Troubling Answer
subpoints: Habakkuk questions why God tolerates injustice in Judah (1:1-4)
God's answer is shocking: He will use the wicked Babylonians to judge His own people (1:5-11)
The prophet is left in anguish — this is not the answer he expected
Habakkuk 2:4 provides the theological anchor: the just shall live by faith — quoted in Romans 1:17, Galatians, and Hebrews

outline.title: II. Fear and Trembling Before God's Revelation (Habakkuk 3:16)
subpoints: When I heard, my body trembled; my lips quivered at the voice — a physical response to the holiness and power of God
Rottenness entered my bones, and I trembled in myself — the proper human response to encountering God's majesty
That I might rest in the day of trouble — even in fear there is a posture of waiting and trust
Authentic faith does not suppress trembling before God's word but rests through it

outline.title: III. Remembering God's Past Deliverances as Foundation for Present Faith
subpoints: Habakkuk 3:1-15 is a majestic recounting of God's mighty acts in the Exodus and through history
Remembering what God has done is the fuel for trusting Him when He seems silent or His ways are troubling
The believer anchors confidence in God's character and track record, not in present circumstances
God's past deliverance is the guarantee of future deliverance

outline.title: IV. Radical Trust Despite Total Desolation (Habakkuk 3:17)
subpoints: Though the fig tree does not blossom, nor fruit be on the vines — agricultural failure complete
The labor of the olive may fail, and the fields yield no food — every source of provision cut off
The flock be cut off from the fold, and no herd in the stalls — total economic disaster
Habakkuk is not trusting God because things are going well — he commits trust before circumstances improve

outline.title: V. Joy in the God of Salvation (Habakkuk 3:18)
subpoints: Yet I will rejoice in the Lord; I will joy in the God of my salvation
Joy is a deliberate choice grounded in the character of God, not in favorable circumstances
The God of salvation — Yeshua, the saving God — is the object of this joy
This is the answer to Habakkuk's complaint: God's ways are higher, and the just shall live by faith (2:4)

outline.title: VI. Strength from the Sovereign Lord (Habakkuk 3:19)
subpoints: Yahweh God is my strength; He will make my feet like deer's feet — sure-footed in the heights
He will make me walk on my high hills — the high place is a place of security and triumph
The strength for living through desolation is not self-generated but received from God
The same God who delivered in the past will carry His people through present troubles

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro expounds Habakkuk 3:16-19, the climactic conclusion of the prophet's dialogue with God. The sermon is set against the backdrop of Habakkuk's double complaint: first, that God is tolerating injustice within Judah (1:1-4), and then, more shockingly, that God intends to use the pagan Babylonians — a nation more wicked than Judah — as His instrument of judgment. The theological answer God gives in 2:4 — the just shall live by faith — becomes the bedrock upon which the final doxology of chapter 3 is built.

The passage moves from fear to faith to joy in three steps. Habakkuk begins in physical trembling at the hearing of God's word (3:16), a response the sermon affirms as appropriate — authentic faith does not suppress trembling before God's majesty but rests through it. He then looks back at God's history of deliverance in 3:1-15, using the memory of past acts as fuel for present trust. The sermon's central challenge comes from the radical either-or of 3:17: even if every source of material provision fails completely — no crops, no flocks, no harvest — yet Habakkuk commits to joy. This is not a conditional trust that waits for circumstances to improve but a deliberate, faith-fueled choice grounded in the character of God as Savior. The sermon closes with the image of deer's feet on the high hills — sure-footed strength that comes not from the prophet's own resources but from Yahweh Himself.
transcript:
You noticed this is not First John. I hope that you noticed that. We are going through First John, but he read from Habakkuk. First John 5:6 and following presents a particular challenge — there is a particular element in it that I want a little more wisdom before preaching on it. So I am saving it, Lord willing, for next week. This morning I want us to look at Habakkuk, and especially verses 16 through 19 of chapter 3. But it is important that we read the whole chapter so that those verses can be placed in context.

You read it — it is confusing. You don't get the warm fuzzies from reading it. It is no Isaiah 53. It is no Psalm 23. None of those more easy-to-understand passages. But I think we can benefit from it as we consider it this morning.

## I. The Love of God Demonstrated in the Cross

There are some events in life that cause your lips to quiver or your knees to knock. We see that in Habakkuk's experience in verse 16. Let me read just that verse from the English Standard Version:

> *"I hear, and my body trembles; my lips quiver at the sound; rottenness enters into my bones; my legs tremble beneath me. Yet I will quietly wait for the day of trouble to come upon people who invade us."* (Habakkuk 3:16)

His lips are quivering. His legs are shaking. His knees are knocking — because of what he heard from the Lord.

There are occasions in life when our lips quiver and our knees knock. Perhaps the birth of your first child, or a teenage son saying, "I love you." Perhaps the sight of your bride coming down the aisle. I love looking at the groom as the bride is coming in, because there are so many emotions expressed on the face of the groom. Perhaps the testimony of what God has done in the life of a sister or brother in Christ causes your lips to quiver. Or perhaps a death in the family, or the news of tragedy.

For Habakkuk, what made his lips quiver and his knees knock was knowing the power of God — the power of God to deliver and the power of God to destroy.

I would like for us to see three things as we look at verses 16 through 19. First, I want us to see the love of God demonstrated in the cross of Jesus Christ. Second, the terror of God demonstrated in the cross of Jesus Christ. And third, faith in God demonstrated in a change of perspective. The first two points are from verse 16, and the last is from verses 17 through 19.

The book of Habakkuk is structured around questions that Habakkuk asked God. First, he was upset with the fact that the people of God — Israel, the church of his time — were not following the Lord. They were taking the Lord's name in vain in all they did. Not just saying wrong things, but they had the name of God upon them while acting as if God were not their God. They were squandering the blessings of the Lord.

Habakkuk came before God and said, "God, how long are you going to let this go? Aren't you going to fix this? Aren't you going to punish or discipline your people? Aren't you going to rescue your name?"

And God says, "Yes, Habakkuk, I'm going to do that. And I'm going to do it by bringing the Chaldeans — that is, the Babylonians — a people more wicked than Israel. They are going to come in and devastate you. That is how I will judge my people."

Habakkuk hears that and says, "Whoa, Lord. I didn't want you to do nothing, but I was thinking more like — no dessert or something like that. But you're going to bring a more wicked nation to punish us? How is that going to go? What are people going to think about you? That you're punishing your own people by means of a more wicked people?"

And God says, "Habakkuk, chapter 2 verse 4: the just shall live by faith." What He means is: Habakkuk, you are just, you are a righteous man. You have faith in Me. You believe that the Messiah is coming to rescue you. So live according to that faith. That faith tells you that I know what I'm doing. I will do what is right, whether you see it or not.

That is when you come to verse 16. When God answered Habakkuk's questions by saying that the just will live by faith, Habakkuk turned his thoughts to the greatest display of God's redemptive power to date. What do you think verses 3 through 15 are about? We read them together. What is those verses about? It talks about the rivers being divided, about armies being drowned by the ocean, about God having something against the rivers and the seas — splitting them. That is a reference to the Exodus. Not only the Exodus, but the conquering of the Promised Land.

In the mind of the Old Testament saint, the Exodus was the greatest display of God's redemptive power. God had delivered His people from the bondage of slavery in Egypt. God mercifully revealed Himself and gave them an identity by way of a covenant at Sinai. God purged them of their sins in the wilderness wanderings. God took them to a place of blessing He had prepared for them in the Promised Land. The Exodus marked the beginning of God's constant, invisible presence with His people, and they treasured that.

So when the Old Testament saint wanted to know if God loved him — if an Old Testament saint wanted to know if God loved her — they would look to God's work of redemption in the Exodus. When they wanted to know if God was going to deliver them again, they would again look to God's work of redemption in the Exodus. They would look at Sinai and God's promise to be a God to them and to their children.

As Habakkuk remembered God's deliverance in verses 3 through 15 — His redemption from slavery and redemption unto freedom and fellowship with God — his lips quivered. The deep emotion coming from within was based on the fact that he knew God as the Deliverer. His knees were knocking, his lips were quivering. Belief, drive, and emotion — knowing God and what He had done — came over him like a flood.

For the saints in the Old Testament, the Exodus was that sign of God's love. For us today, the cross is the major, ultimate, and glorious redemptive event in the history of God's people. The deliverance of God's people from Egypt was just a shadow pointing to the ultimate deliverance of God's people from His wrath and from sin in the cross of Jesus Christ.

The cross is the focal, the central, the most important event in the history of salvation. Beyond that, it is the greatest demonstration of God's love for His people.

So when you want to know that God loves you, what do you do? Do you look at the grandest demonstration of that love — the most objective, unquestionable demonstration of God's love for you — the cross of Jesus Christ? That is where we need to look, because it is objective. It cannot be questioned.

In all honesty, life is often very challenging — or to say it in more theological terms, life often stinks. Tragedy, heartbreaks, disappointments can be part of living, and more often than not they are part of living. Marriages in bad shape, kids who rebel, life struggles, death, famine, old age, wars, wickedness all around. We look around and we are tempted to wonder if God really loves us.

But when we are tempted to do that, we need to take a hike up a little hill just outside of Jerusalem and see the cross of Christ on the hill of Calvary. Because on that hill, God crucified His Son because He loved us. There is nothing going on around us that can undo what God did for us on the cross of Calvary. He endured the cross for the joy set before Him — that is, His people.

Because of that, there is nothing that can separate us from the love of God in Jesus Christ. Do you believe that? We just recited it. We affirmed it in our responsive reading of Romans 8:

> *"Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword? No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. For I am sure that neither death nor life nor angels nor rulers nor things present nor things to come nor powers nor height nor depth nor anything else in all creation will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord."* (Romans 8:35–39)

You can add whatever you want to that list — it does not have the power to separate you from the love of God in Jesus Christ. Why? Because of the cross of Jesus Christ. All of that for red-jured, undeserving sinners such as us.

I think we often struggle with contentment and joy because I think we often struggle with contentment and joy because we think we deserve something. Not even something better — just something. And yet the Scripture describes us as a worm — dead in trespasses and sins, going the opposite direction. And yet God in His goodness and love rescued us through the cross of Calvary. And nothing can separate us from Christ.

God the Son stood condemned so that we could stand free. We didn't read this part, but in Romans 8:31–32 Paul says:

> *"What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, how will He not also with Him graciously give us all things?"*

All He requires of us is that we believe. The Philippian jailer asked Paul and Silas, "What must I do to be saved?" And Paul and Silas told him, "Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ and you will be saved, you and your household."

So in the cross of Christ we have the love of God demonstrated.

## II. The Terror of God Demonstrated in the Cross

We also have the terror of God demonstrated in the cross of Christ. Look again at verse 16:

> *"I hear, and my body trembles; my lips quiver at the sound; rottenness enters into my bones; my legs tremble beneath me. Yet I will quietly wait for the day of trouble to come upon people who invade us."*

The reality is that as Habakkuk thought about verses 3 through 15, he also saw the power of God in judgment. If God could destroy Egypt's army — the most powerful army in existence at the time — how much more will He do to this tiny nation at the hands of the Babylonians? What is Babylon going to do with this tiny little nation that has no power, when they are coming with the most powerful army of the time? The weight of God's judgment made Habakkuk's knees knock.

We should have a similar reaction when we think of the cross, because the cross speaks not only of God's love but also of God's judgment. When you look at the cross, you must see the terror of it — the Father judging His Son for sin. The terror of the wrath of God being poured upon Jesus. The separation of the Son from the Father — the cry, "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" The hell that the cross was. God Himself paying for the sins of His people, the just for the unjust.

It is a grotesque picture, if we are going to be honest. That is why Paul calls it the offense of the cross. That is why some have wrongly called it cosmic child abuse. It is a grotesque picture — the wrath of God poured upon His own Son.

And when you look at the cross, you should see the ugliness of sin. The marred face of Jesus, the wounds, the nails, the crown of thorns, the bleeding back — that is all your sins doing. We are talking about sin collectively putting Jesus on the cross. But if you had been the only person to ever live after Adam, it would have taken the same cross to redeem just you. So you can very personally say: my sins put Him on the cross.

John Newton says:

> *I saw one hanging on a tree, in agony and blood,
> Who fixed His loving eyes on me, as near His cross I stood.*

Every time you look at the cross, remember you are charged with Jesus' death. But Newton continues:

> *My conscience felt and owned the guilt, and plunged me in despair;
> I saw my sins His blood had spilt, and helped to nail Him there.*

But with a second look He said:

> *I freely all forgive; this blood is for your ransom paid;
> I died that you might live.*

And then Newton concludes:

> *Thus while His death my sin displays for all the world to view,
> Such is the mystery of grace: it seals my pardon too.
> With pleasing grief and mournful joy my spirit now is filled,
> That I should such a life destroy, yet live by Him I killed.*

So we look at the cross and we see the wrath of God. But we see the wrath of God being appeased by the love of God. The cross demonstrates the love of God; the cross demonstrates the terror of God. And as we embrace both of them in faith, we will have a faith that is demonstrated in a change of perspective.

## III. Faith in God Demonstrated in a Change of Perspective

Look at verses 17 through 19:

> *"Though the fig tree may not blossom, nor fruit be on the vines; though the labor of the olive may fail and the fields yield no food; though the flock may be cut off from the fold, and there be no herd in the stalls — yet I will rejoice in the Lord, I will joy in the God of my salvation. The Lord God is my strength; He will make my feet like deer's feet, and He will make me walk on my high hills."* (Habakkuk 3:17–19)

Knowing that God is his Deliverer, Habakkuk is able to face this life with all its difficulties. Nothing has changed other than this: he gazed upon the Lord. That is the only change we have here from the rest of the book. He gazed upon the Lord.

Now, with a much different attitude than he had at the beginning of the book, he comes before the Lord. He is not demanding an answer anymore. He comes in trust. He says: even if all these bad things happen — the fig tree not blossoming, the vine not producing fruit, the olive not producing oil — if all this happens, I'm going to trust, because the just shall live by faith.

Verse 17 describes why Habakkuk can quietly wait for the day of trouble. And Habakkuk's new commitment is to rejoice in the Lord. Once he saw the beauty of God — the love of God, the terror of God — he decides, "I'm going to rejoice in the Lord." It is a commitment he makes.

We see that in verse 18: "I will rejoice in the Lord. I will joy in the God of my salvation." And notice that he is going to rejoice in God because He is the God of his salvation. He understands that he doesn't need anything else if God is his salvation. It doesn't matter if the Babylonians are coming to destroy him tomorrow, because God is the God of his salvation. It doesn't matter if everything is taken from him, because God is the God of his salvation.

Is that enough for you? Is God your salvation enough? I urge you to be satisfied with the God who saved you through Jesus Christ — who poured the guilt of your sins upon Him. Be satisfied knowing that the love of God is never going to depart from you. Be satisfied knowing that there is nothing that can separate you from the love of God.

Habakkuk is now able to echo the Apostle Paul:

> *"Whatever gain I had, I counted as loss for the sake of Christ. Indeed, I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord. For His sake I have suffered the loss of all things and count them as rubbish, in order that I may gain Christ and be found in Him, not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that depends on faith — that I may know Him and the power of His resurrection, and may share His sufferings, becoming like Him in His death."* (Philippians 3:7–10)

That is what Habakkuk says in verse 18: "Yet I will rejoice in the Lord. I will joy in the God of my salvation."

Can you say that with the Apostle Paul? Christ has given the grace to count everything — good things, bad things, sufferings, whatever it is — as loss, that we may gain Christ. Do we want to gain Christ?

Because of that, Habakkuk's strength is renewed, for now the Lord is his strength — verse 19: "The Lord God is my strength." Not the standing of Israel. Not sufferings. Nothing in this life is his strength. The Lord God is his strength. Yahweh, who delivered Israel from Egypt — the Lord is going to lift him, because he is not trusting in anything else. And he doesn't need anything else.

Habakkuk has finally humbled himself. And the Scripture says: "Humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God, that at the proper time He may exalt you. Cast all your anxieties on Him, because He cares for you."

Do you notice that nothing changed in Habakkuk's circumstances? Nothing, absolutely nothing. The Babylonians are still coming. They are still going to come, and it is going to be a death of suffering. They are going to be in captivity for seventy years, away from the land that God had given them. All of that is true. The only thing that changed was that he took his eyes off of the here and now and placed them on his Redeemer. That is the only thing that changed in this whole passage.

What is it that Habakkuk is trusting in? Where is his gaze?

Are you ready to do that? Are you ready to take your eyes off of the present, the here and now, and look to Christ who is coming again for you? Are you ready to not live life as permanent citizens of this place, but as pilgrims and sojourners of the life to come? Are you ready to see the beauty of your Savior face to face, because you will be like Him when He comes back?

The Lord says to the prophet in Isaiah 26:3:

> *"You keep him in perfect peace whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you."*

This experience of keeping our minds upon Jesus is well expressed by the Puritan Samuel Rutherford. In a letter to a parishioner he said: "Since He looked upon me" — talking about Christ — "since Christ looked upon me, my heart is not my own. He that is Christ has run away to heaven with it."

Is your heart in heaven? Has Christ run away with your heart?
